title: Compose Advanced
description: LazyColumn, side effects, animations, and performance

references.title: Android Developers — Side Effects in Compose

lesson:
Side Effects — The Complete Reference

Side effects are operations that escape the scope of a composable function: network calls, logging, writing to a database, showing a toast. Compose provides specific APIs for each pattern:

APIWhen It RunsUse ForProvides Cleanup?
LaunchedEffect(key)On enter + when key changesOne-shot or keyed coroutines (fetch data, show snackbar)Cancelled automatically
DisposableEffect(key)On enter + when key changesRegister/unregister patterns (observers, listeners, callbacks)Yes — onDispose { }
SideEffectAfter every successful recompositionSync Compose state to non-Compose code (analytics, logging)No
produceState(initial)On enter (creates State<T>)Convert non-Compose async sources (Flow, callback) to Compose StateCancelled automatically
derivedStateOf { }When source state changesComputed state that only recomposes when result changesN/A
rememberCoroutineScope()Returns scope for event handlersLaunch coroutines from callbacks (onClick, etc.)Cancelled on leave

LaunchedEffect — Keyed Coroutines

// Runs ONCE when composable enters composition (key = Unit) LaunchedEffect(Unit) { viewModel.loadData() } // Re-launches when userId changes LaunchedEffect(userId) { val user = api.fetchUser(userId) // cancelled + restarted on new userId userName = user.name } // Show snackbar (suspend function) on error LaunchedEffect(errorMessage) { errorMessage?.let { snackbarHostState.showSnackbar(it) } }

DisposableEffect — Setup + Cleanup

// Register a lifecycle observer — MUST clean up DisposableEffect(lifecycleOwner) { val observer = LifecycleEventObserver { _, event -> if (event == Lifecycle.Event.ON_RESUME) isActive = true } lifecycleOwner.lifecycle.addObserver(observer) onDispose { lifecycleOwner.lifecycle.removeObserver(observer) // cleanup! } } // Register a BroadcastReceiver DisposableEffect(Unit) { val receiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() { override fun onReceive(ctx: Context, intent: Intent) { /* handle */ } } context.registerReceiver(receiver, IntentFilter(Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED)) onDispose { context.unregisterReceiver(receiver) } }

rememberCoroutineScope — For Event Handlers

// LaunchedEffect runs on composition — but what about onClick? // You can't call suspend functions directly in onClick. // Use rememberCoroutineScope() instead: @Composable fun ScrollToTopButton(listState: LazyListState) { val scope = rememberCoroutineScope() Button(onClick = { scope.launch { listState.animateScrollToItem(0) } // suspend fun in event handler }) { Text("Scroll to top") } }
LaunchedEffect vs rememberCoroutineScope: Use LaunchedEffect for effects tied to composition lifecycle (runs automatically). Use rememberCoroutineScope for effects triggered by user events (onClick, onDrag). Never use rememberCoroutineScope to launch coroutines during composition — that's what LaunchedEffect is for.

produceState — Bridge Non-Compose Sources

// Convert a suspend function to Compose State @Composable fun UserAvatar(userId: String): State<UserImage?> { return produceState<UserImage?>(initialValue = null, userId) { value = api.fetchAvatar(userId) // suspend call → State<T> } }

CompositionLocal — Implicit Data Passing

CompositionLocal passes data down the composition tree implicitly, without threading it through every function parameter. Think of it like Android's Context — available everywhere without explicit passing.

// Built-in CompositionLocals you use constantly: val context = LocalContext.current // Android Context val density = LocalDensity.current // Screen density val config = LocalConfiguration.current // Screen size, orientation val lifecycleOwner = LocalLifecycleOwner.current val view = LocalView.current // Creating your own CompositionLocal: val LocalUserTheme = compositionLocalOf<UserTheme> { error("No UserTheme provided") // default if not provided } // Providing a value down the tree: CompositionLocalProvider(LocalUserTheme provides darkTheme) { // Everything inside here sees darkTheme MyScreen() // can call LocalUserTheme.current } // Reading the value anywhere in the subtree: @Composable fun ThemedButton() { val theme = LocalUserTheme.current Button(colors = ButtonDefaults.buttonColors(containerColor = theme.primary)) { Text("Themed") } }
compositionLocalOf vs staticCompositionLocalOf: Use compositionLocalOf for values that change (recomposes only readers). Use staticCompositionLocalOf for values that rarely change (recomposes entire subtree on change, but more efficient when stable). Most built-in locals use staticCompositionLocalOf.

LazyColumn Deep Dive

LazyColumn is the Compose equivalent of RecyclerView — it only composes and lays out visible items. But getting it right requires attention to keys, content types, and performance.

LazyColumn( modifier = Modifier.fillMaxSize(), contentPadding = PaddingValues(16.dp), // padding around all content verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp) // spacing between items ) { // key: binds remembered state to identity, not position // contentType: enables smarter item reuse (like RecyclerView viewType) items( items = messages, key = { it.id }, // CRITICAL for correct state + animations contentType = { it.type } // "text", "image", "header" → better reuse ) { message -> MessageCard(message) } // Sticky headers (experimental) stickyHeader { Text("Section A", modifier = Modifier.background(Color.LightGray).fillMaxWidth().padding(8.dp)) } // Single item (for headers, footers, loading indicators) item { LoadingIndicator() } }
LazyColumn Performance Tips:
1. Always provide keys — without them, state follows position during scrolling (wrong checkboxes checked).
2. Use contentType — helps Compose reuse compositions between items of the same type.
3. Avoid nesting LazyColumn inside a scrollable Column — use a single LazyColumn with different item types.
4. Keep item composables lightweight — heavy computation in items blocks the UI thread.
5. Use Modifier.animateItem() — animate additions/removals/reorderings (requires keys).

Compose Navigation

Jetpack Navigation Compose manages the back stack and screen transitions declaratively:

// Basic NavHost setup val navController = rememberNavController() NavHost(navController, startDestination = "home") { composable("home") { HomeScreen(onItemClick = { id -> navController.navigate("detail/$id") }) } composable( route = "detail/{id}", arguments = listOf(navArgument("id") { type = NavType.IntType }) ) { backStackEntry -> val id = backStackEntry.arguments?.getInt("id") ?: 0 DetailScreen(id) } } // Type-safe navigation (2.8+ with Kotlin Serialization): @Serializable data class Detail(val id: Int) @Serializable data object Home NavHost(navController, startDestination = Home) { composable<Home> { HomeScreen() } composable<Detail> { backStackEntry -> val detail: Detail = backStackEntry.toRoute() DetailScreen(detail.id) // compile-time type safety! } }

View <-> Compose Interop

In real apps, you migrate incrementally. Compose and Views must coexist:

// Compose inside a View (Fragment/Activity): class MyFragment : Fragment() { override fun onCreateView(...) = ComposeView(requireContext()).apply { setViewCompositionStrategy( ViewCompositionStrategy.DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed ) setContent { MaterialTheme { MyComposableScreen() } } } } // View inside Compose (wrapping legacy Views): @Composable fun LegacyMapView() { AndroidView( factory = { context -> MapView(context).apply { onCreate(null) } }, update = { mapView -> mapView.moveCamera(...) } // called on recomposition ) }
ViewCompositionStrategy controls when Compose disposes. Use DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed for Fragments (disposes when Fragment view is destroyed). The default DisposeOnDetachedFromWindow can cause issues in ViewPager2 or Fragment transitions.

Animations

Compose makes animations declarative — describe the target state, Compose handles the transition:

// 1. AnimatedVisibility — show/hide with animation AnimatedVisibility( visible = isVisible, enter = fadeIn() + slideInVertically(), exit = fadeOut() + slideOutVertically() ) { Text("I animate in and out!") } // 2. animate*AsState — animate a single value val alpha by animateFloatAsState( targetValue = if (isSelected) 1f else 0.3f, animationSpec = tween(300) ) Text("Fading", modifier = Modifier.alpha(alpha)) // 3. animateContentSize — animate size changes Column(modifier = Modifier.animateContentSize()) { Text("Title") if (expanded) Text("Long description content...") } // 4. Crossfade — crossfade between composables based on state Crossfade(targetState = currentTab) { tab -> when (tab) { Tab.Home -> HomeScreen() Tab.Settings -> SettingsScreen() } } // 5. updateTransition — coordinate multiple animations val transition = updateTransition(targetState = selected, label = "select") val borderColor by transition.animateColor(label = "border") { if (it) Color.Blue else Color.Gray } val elevation by transition.animateDp(label = "elevation") { if (it) 8.dp else 2.dp }
Animation Decision Tree: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Show/hide content? → AnimatedVisibility Animate a single value? → animate*AsState (Float, Color, Dp, Int, etc.) Size changes? → Modifier.animateContentSize() Swap content? → Crossfade or AnimatedContent Multiple coordinated? → updateTransition Infinite/repeating? → rememberInfiniteTransition Full custom? → Animatable + LaunchedEffect

Recomposition Performance

Compose can skip recomposition of a composable when all parameters are stable and unchanged. Understanding stability is key to performance:

Stable types (Compose can skip recomposition): ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ✓ Primitives: Int, Float, Boolean, String ✓ data class with only stable fields ✓ @Immutable classes (promise: never changes) ✓ @Stable classes (promise: changes are observable) ✓ Function types (lambdas — if captured values are stable) Unstable types (Compose ALWAYS recomposes): ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ✗ List, Set, Map (mutable interfaces) ✗ Classes from external modules (Compose can't verify) ✗ data class with any unstable field Fix: Use @Immutable annotation or kotlinx.collections.immutable

questions.title: LazyColumn Item Reuse Bug
questions.description: This LazyColumn shows a list of messages, but when you scroll, the wrong items show checked states. Fix the issue.

checks.message: Add a key parameter to items() to uniquely identify each item

questions.explanation: Without a key, LazyColumn associates remember state with position, not identity. When items are recycled during scrolling, state stays at the position. Adding key = { it.id } binds state to each message's unique ID.
hints: When items move or recycle, how does Compose know which state belongs to which item?

questions.title: Unnecessary Recomposition
questions.description: This composable recomposes the entire list every time the timestamp updates (every second). Optimize it so only the timestamp text recomposes.

checks.message: Extract the timestamp into a separate composable

questions.explanation: When currentTime changes, Compose recomposes any composable reading it. By extracting the timestamp into its own composable, only TimestampHeader recomposes. This is the principle of narrow recomposition scope.
hints: Which state is changing frequently? Which part of the UI needs it?
Compose recomposes at the composable function boundary

questions.title: LaunchedEffect
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to show a snackbar when an error occurs.

blanks.hint: Side effect that launches a coroutine
Key that triggers re-launch when changed

questions.explanation: LaunchedEffect(key) launches a coroutine when entering composition and re-launches when the key changes. showSnackbar() is a suspend function, requiring the coroutine scope.
hints: This side effect runs a coroutine tied to composition lifecycle

questions.title: Side Effects in Compose
questions.description: Which side effect API should you use to register and unregister a broadcast receiver while the composable is in the composition?
questions.explanation: DisposableEffect is designed for effects needing cleanup — it provides an onDispose block. Perfect for register/unregister patterns. LaunchedEffect is for coroutines. SideEffect runs after every recomposition with no cleanup.
hints: Which effect gives you a cleanup callback?

questions.title: derivedStateOf
questions.description: What does derivedStateOf do in this code?
options: Caches computation and only triggers recomposition when its result changes (true/false), not on every scroll pixel
Runs the computation on a background thread
Same as remember{} — just caches the initial value
Delays computation until layout is complete
questions.explanation: derivedStateOf creates derived state that only triggers recomposition when its result changes. Without it, reading scroll position directly would recompose on every pixel. With it, the boolean only changes when crossing the threshold, dramatically reducing recompositions.
hints: Think about how often scroll position changes vs how often the boolean changes

questions.title: AnimatedVisibility
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to animate a panel sliding in and out based on a boolean state.
questions.explanation: AnimatedVisibility animates the appearance and disappearance of its content based on a boolean. By default it uses fade + expand animations. You can customize with enter and exit parameters.
hints: This composable handles the animation automatically based on a boolean
The boolean state controls whether content is shown or hidden

questions.title: DisposableEffect Missing Cleanup
questions.description: This code registers a lifecycle observer but never removes it, causing a memory leak. Fix it.

checks.message: Clean up the observer in onDispose

questions.explanation: LaunchedEffect provides no cleanup mechanism. DisposableEffect provides onDispose { } which runs when the composable leaves composition or the key changes. Always remove observers, unregister receivers, and close resources in onDispose.
hints: LaunchedEffect is for coroutines, not register/unregister patterns
What happens to the observer when this composable is removed from the tree?

questions.title: Composition over Inheritance
questions.description: Why does Jetpack Compose use composition (small composable functions) instead of inheritance (extending View classes)?
options: Composition allows mixing and matching behaviors freely without rigid class hierarchies
Composition was easier for Google to implement
questions.explanation: Inheritance creates rigid hierarchies — you can't have a Button that's also a TextField. Composition lets you freely combine behaviors: wrap any content in any container, add any modifier to any composable. A Button is just a composable that takes content as a lambda — the content can be anything. This is far more flexible than the old View class hierarchy.
hints: Think about the old View system: Button extends TextView extends View — what if you want something that doesn't fit?
